title: Vancomycin — Dosing & Monitoring
oneLiner: IV antibiotic for serious Gram‑positive infections (esp. MRSA); requires TDM to balance efficacy against nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Red man syndrome (non‑IgE histamine release):** sudden hypotension, erythema, pruritus during infusion – **the single finding is onset within 30 minutes of starting the infusion** and rapid resolution when infusion is stopped/slowed.  
- **Acute kidney injury (AKI) from supratherapeutic trough:** usually develops over 24–72 h rather than first hour, but if a patient already has high troughs from a previous dose (e.g. >20 mg/L) and you give another dose without checking, you can precipitate oliguric AKI – **the single finding is a rising creatinine >26 μmol/L within 24 h** in the setting of a trough >15–20 mg/L.
sections.Presentation (Clinical Context for Vanco:
- Suspected/confirmed **MRSA** infection (bloodstream, pneumonia, skin/soft tissue, bone/joint, meningitis, endocarditis)
- **Severe sepsis** where Gram‑positive cover is needed (e.g. septic arthritis, prosthetic joint infection, central line‑associated infection)
- Empiric therapy in **neutropenic sepsis** (if local epidemiology favours MRSA)
- **Penicillin/cephalosporin allergy** with serious Gram‑positive infection
- Key **exam findings** are those of the underlying infection (e.g. fever, hypotension, focal signs) – vancomycin itself has no unique exam findings
sections.Differential Diagnosis (Alternative Anti:
- **Teicoplanin** – similar spectrum, less nephrotoxic, no TDM in many hospitals [check local policy]; good alternative for endocarditis or when once‑daily dosing desired; more expensive
- **Daptomycin** – rapid bactericidal, once‑daily, but **inactivated by lung surfactant** → do NOT use for pneumonia; use for MRSA bacteraemia, endocarditis, skin infections
- **Linezolid** – oral and IV, good lung penetration, no renal adjustment; but **myelosuppression** (thrombocytopenia) with >14 days; avoid with MAOIs
- **Ceftaroline** – anti‑MRSA cephalosporin, good for skin & soft tissue, not first‑line for endocarditis; rare C. diff
- **Splitter:** Pneumonia → avoid daptomycin; Linezolid gives better lung penetration; Vancomycin is fine but requires TDM; If the patient has **severe sepsis with acute kidney injury**, consider teicoplanin or daptomycin (less nephrotoxic) rather than vancomycin.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Check IV access (must be running well, no extravasation); Assess for red man syndrome during the **first** infusion – note any flushing or hypotension.  
- **Bloods:**  
  - Baseline U&Es, creatinine (eGFR) – **essential before first dose**  
  - Vancomycin **trough level** – take immediately before the **4th dose** (if conventional intermittent dosing); for critically ill or rapidly changing renal function, may need earlier level [check local policy]  
  - FBC (monitor for neutropenia with prolonged therapy), LFTs (baseline)  
- **Imaging:** Not required for vancomycin itself – dictated by the focus of infection.  
- **Special tests:**  
  - If endocarditis suspected → echocardiography, blood cultures (2–3 sets) before antibiotics  
  - If meningitis → CSF culture and vancomycin concentration in CSF (therapeutic target ~10 mg/L)  
- **Never‑miss test:** **Vancomycin trough level before the 4th dose** – omitting this is the single most common error that leads to AKI or sub‑therapeutic dosing. Also **always check baseline creatinine** before starting therapy.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Confirm patient has **actual body weight** (dose based on total body weight; for obese patients some protocols use adjusted or ideal body weight – [check local policy])  
  - Give **loading dose** if critically ill (e.g. sepsis, known MRSA): BNF suggests 25–30 mg/kg (max 2.5 g) infused over 60–120 min [check local policy for exact load]  
  - Start **maintenance regimen** based on renal function: e.g. 15–20 mg/kg IV every 12 h if eGFR >60 mL/min; adjust interval if lower (see Drugs & Doses)  
  - **Infusion rate:** always over ≥60 min; maximum rate 10 mg/min – slower if previous red man syndrome  
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**  
  - Check **trough level** before the 4th dose, then adjust dose/interval accordingly  
  - Re‑check trough and creatinine after each dose change (usually after 2–3 doses)  
  - Daily U&Es while on IV therapy  
  - For **prolonged therapy (>7 days)**: FBC weekly (neutropenia risk) and audiology if symptoms (tinnitus, hearing loss)  
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**  
  - If ≥4 weeks therapy (e.g. osteomyelitis, endocarditis), consider **teicoplanin** or **oral linezolid** to reduce line days and monitoring burden  
  - Outpatient IV therapy: arrange home TDM service, monitor renal function weekly  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride @ 1 mL/kg/h if not on fluid restriction (adjust per clinical need)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard – dateparin 5000 units SC daily (adjust for weight/renal)  
  - Diet: nil‑by‑mouth only if endocarditis (pre‑op) or GI pathology; otherwise normal diet  
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2, then per local policy (usually 4‑hourly for stable ward patients)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First‑line agent:** Vancomycin (as vancomycin hydrochloride for IV infusion)  
  - **Loading dose (if used):** 25–30 mg/kg IV (max 2.5 g) over 60–120 min – [check local policy]  
  - **Maintenance dose (conventional every 12 h) – for eGFR >60 mL/min:  
    - 15–20 mg/kg IV every 12 h (max 2 g/day)  
    - **Target trough concentration:**  
      - Uncomplicated: 10–15 mg/L  
      - Severe/complicated (endocarditis, meningitis, osteomyelitis, pneumonia): 15–20 mg/L  
  - **Renal impairment:**  
    - eGFR 30–60 mL/min: 15–20 mg/kg IV every 24–48 h [check local nomogram]  
    - eGFR <30 mL/min: 15–20 mg/kg IV every 48–96 h or use anuric dosing (single dose with subsequent levels) – **[check local policy]**  
    - Haemodialysis: give dose after dialysis, target trough <10–15 mg/L; may need very low or no maintenance depending on residual function  
  - **Hepatic impairment:** no dose adjustment required (vancomycin is renally eliminated)  
- **Common alternatives:** teicoplanin, daptomycin, linezolid, ceftaroline – see Differential  
- **Key contraindications:** previous anaphylaxis to vancomycin; caution in pregnancy (B‑lactam alternatives preferred)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving a dose without checking the latest trough level** – especially in patients with rising creatinine – this is the commonest cause of preventable AKI. The second most dangerous is **rapid infusion (<60 min)** causing severe red man syndrome with hypotension.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Red man syndrome (histamine release) – treat by slowing infusion, antihistamines (chlorphenamine 10 mg IV/IM), rarely need adrenaline  
  - Acute kidney injury – directly proportional to high troughs (>20 mg/L)  
  - Phlebitis / thrombophlebitis – rotate IV sites, consider central line  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Ototoxicity (tinnitus, high‑frequency hearing loss) – uncommon with modern preparations, risk increases with cumulative dose and co‑administration of other ototoxins (e.g. gentamicin)  
  - Neutropenia (usually after >2 weeks therapy) – reversible on cessation
sections.Prognosis: - Vancomycin is effective but **time‑dependent** – maintaining trough >10 mg/L correlates with improved outcomes for MRSA bloodstream infections. Trough >20 mg/L increases AKI risk. Mortality for MRSA bacteraemia ~20–30% with appropriate therapy, higher if source control inadequate.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always use actual body weight for the first dose** – underdosing is a common error in obese patients; after loading, you can adjust based on levels.  
- **Give a loading dose in critically ill patients** – this achieves therapeutic levels faster (by 12–24 hours) and reduces time to clinical response.  
- **Check a trough before the 4th dose** – this is the least disruptive way to get a steady‑state level; if the patient is unstable, check an earlier level (e.g. after 2–3 doses) and [check local policy].
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT skip the loading dose in severe sepsis** – you will have sub‑therapeutic levels for >12 hours.  
- **Do NOT wait for the trough result before giving the second dose** – the first dose is given, and the second dose may be due before the level is back. Give it unless you suspect toxicity (check clinical signs, creatinine).  
- **Do NOT use trough monitoring alone if you switch to continuous infusion** – for continuous infusion the target is a steady‑state level of ~15–25 mg/L, not a pre‑dose trough – consult local pharmacy.  
- **Do NOT co‑administer with other nephrotoxic drugs (gentamicin, furosemide, NSAIDs) without intensified monitoring** – risk of AKI increases.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home on:
- **Return if:**  
  - Any new rash, fever, or hypotension during infusion (may be red man syndrome or allergic reaction)  
  - Decreased urine output (oliguria), dark urine, or oedema (may indicate AKI)  
  - Tinnitus, hearing loss, or dizziness  
  - Signs of line sepsis (redness, pain, swelling at IV site)  
- **Follow‑up:**  
  - Weekly U&Es and trough levels (or as per local TDM service)  
  - Weekly FBC if on therapy >7 days  
  - Audiology referral if ototoxic symptoms develop
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior clinician or clinical pharmacist immediately for:**  
  - Trough >20 mg/L (requires dose withholding and review)  
  - Creatinine rise >50% from baseline (or >26 μmol/L in 24 h)  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (new hypotension, tachypnoea)  
  - Anaphylaxis to vancomycin (use adrenaline 0.5 mg IM)  
  - GCS <15 or new focal neurology (possible meningitis)  
- **Consider ICU referral if:**  
  - Shock requiring vasopressors despite adequate fluid resuscitation  
  - Rapidly progressing AKI (e.g. need for dialysis)  
  - Refractory seizures (consider vancomycin‑induced encephalopathy – rare but dose‑related)

rawMarkdown:
# Vancomycin – Dosing & Monitoring
**One-liner:** IV antibiotic for serious Gram‑positive infections (esp. MRSA); requires TDM to balance efficacy against nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Red man syndrome (non‑IgE histamine release):** sudden hypotension, erythema, pruritus during infusion – **the single finding is onset within 30 minutes of starting the infusion** and rapid resolution when infusion is stopped/slowed.  
- **Acute kidney injury (AKI) from supratherapeutic trough:** usually develops over 24–72 h rather than first hour, but if a patient already has high troughs from a previous dose (e.g. >20 mg/L) and you give another dose without checking, you can precipitate oliguric AKI – **the single finding is a rising creatinine >26 μmol/L within 24 h** in the setting of a trough >15–20 mg/L.

### Presentation (Clinical Context for Vancomycin Use)
- Suspected/confirmed **MRSA** infection (bloodstream, pneumonia, skin/soft tissue, bone/joint, meningitis, endocarditis)
- **Severe sepsis** where Gram‑positive cover is needed (e.g. septic arthritis, prosthetic joint infection, central line‑associated infection)
- Empiric therapy in **neutropenic sepsis** (if local epidemiology favours MRSA)
- **Penicillin/cephalosporin allergy** with serious Gram‑positive infection
- Key **exam findings** are those of the underlying infection (e.g. fever, hypotension, focal signs) – vancomycin itself has no unique exam findings

### Differential Diagnosis (Alternative Antibiotics for Severe Gram‑Positive Infections)
- **Teicoplanin** – similar spectrum, less nephrotoxic, no TDM in many hospitals [check local policy]; good alternative for endocarditis or when once‑daily dosing desired; more expensive
- **Daptomycin** – rapid bactericidal, once‑daily, but **inactivated by lung surfactant** → do NOT use for pneumonia; use for MRSA bacteraemia, endocarditis, skin infections
- **Linezolid** – oral and IV, good lung penetration, no renal adjustment; but **myelosuppression** (thrombocytopenia) with >14 days; avoid with MAOIs
- **Ceftaroline** – anti‑MRSA cephalosporin, good for skin & soft tissue, not first‑line for endocarditis; rare C. diff
- **Splitter:** Pneumonia → avoid daptomycin; Linezolid gives better lung penetration; Vancomycin is fine but requires TDM; If the patient has **severe sepsis with acute kidney injury**, consider teicoplanin or daptomycin (less nephrotoxic) rather than vancomycin.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Check IV access (must be running well, no extravasation); Assess for red man syndrome during the **first** infusion – note any flushing or hypotension.  
- **Bloods:**  
  - Baseline U&Es, creatinine (eGFR) – **essential before first dose**  
  - Vancomycin **trough level** – take immediately before the **4th dose** (if conventional intermittent dosing); for critically ill or rapidly changing renal function, may need earlier level [check local policy]  
  - FBC (monitor for neutropenia with prolonged therapy), LFTs (baseline)  
- **Imaging:** Not required for vancomycin itself – dictated by the focus of infection.  
- **Special tests:**  
  - If endocarditis suspected → echocardiography, blood cultures (2–3 sets) before antibiotics  
  - If meningitis → CSF culture and vancomycin concentration in CSF (therapeutic target ~10 mg/L)  
- **Never‑miss test:** **Vancomycin trough level before the 4th dose** – omitting this is the single most common error that leads to AKI or sub‑therapeutic dosing. Also **always check baseline creatinine** before starting therapy.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Confirm patient has **actual body weight** (dose based on total body weight; for obese patients some protocols use adjusted or ideal body weight – [check local policy])  
  - Give **loading dose** if critically ill (e.g. sepsis, known MRSA): BNF suggests 25–30 mg/kg (max 2.5 g) infused over 60–120 min [check local policy for exact load]  
  - Start **maintenance regimen** based on renal function: e.g. 15–20 mg/kg IV every 12 h if eGFR >60 mL/min; adjust interval if lower (see Drugs & Doses)  
  - **Infusion rate:** always over ≥60 min; maximum rate 10 mg/min – slower if previous red man syndrome  
- **Ward (first 24–72 h):**  
  - Check **trough level** before the 4th dose, then adjust dose/interval accordingly  
  - Re‑check trough and creatinine after each dose change (usually after 2–3 doses)  
  - Daily U&Es while on IV therapy  
  - For **prolonged therapy (>7 days)**: FBC weekly (neutropenia risk) and audiology if symptoms (tinnitus, hearing loss)  
- **Long‑term / outpatient:**  
  - If ≥4 weeks therapy (e.g. osteomyelitis, endocarditis), consider **teicoplanin** or **oral linezolid** to reduce line days and monitoring burden  
  - Outpatient IV therapy: arrange home TDM service, monitor renal function weekly  
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  - IV fluids: 0.9% sodium chloride @ 1 mL/kg/h if not on fluid restriction (adjust per clinical need)  
  - VTE prophylaxis: standard – dateparin 5000 units SC daily (adjust for weight/renal)  
  - Diet: nil‑by‑mouth only if endocarditis (pre‑op) or GI pathology; otherwise normal diet  
  - Observations: baseline NEWS2, then per local policy (usually 4‑hourly for stable ward patients)

### Drugs & Doses
- **First‑line agent:** Vancomycin (as vancomycin hydrochloride for IV infusion)  
  - **Loading dose (if used):** 25–30 mg/kg IV (max 2.5 g) over 60–120 min – [check local policy]  
  - **Maintenance dose (conventional every 12 h) – for eGFR >60 mL/min:  
    - 15–20 mg/kg IV every 12 h (max 2 g/day)  
    - **Target trough concentration:**  
      - Uncomplicated: 10–15 mg/L  
      - Severe/complicated (endocarditis, meningitis, osteomyelitis, pneumonia): 15–20 mg/L  
  - **Renal impairment:**  
    - eGFR 30–60 mL/min: 15–20 mg/kg IV every 24–48 h [check local nomogram]  
    - eGFR <30 mL/min: 15–20 mg/kg IV every 48–96 h or use anuric dosing (single dose with subsequent levels) – **[check local policy]**  
    - Haemodialysis: give dose after dialysis, target trough <10–15 mg/L; may need very low or no maintenance depending on residual function  
  - **Hepatic impairment:** no dose adjustment required (vancomycin is renally eliminated)  
- **Common alternatives:** teicoplanin, daptomycin, linezolid, ceftaroline – see Differential  
- **Key contraindications:** previous anaphylaxis to vancomycin; caution in pregnancy (B‑lactam alternatives preferred)  
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Giving a dose without checking the latest trough level** – especially in patients with rising creatinine – this is the commonest cause of preventable AKI. The second most dangerous is **rapid infusion (<60 min)** causing severe red man syndrome with hypotension.

### Complications
- **Acute:**  
  - Red man syndrome (histamine release) – treat by slowing infusion, antihistamines (chlorphenamine 10 mg IV/IM), rarely need adrenaline  
  - Acute kidney injury – directly proportional to high troughs (>20 mg/L)  
  - Phlebitis / thrombophlebitis – rotate IV sites, consider central line  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Ototoxicity (tinnitus, high‑frequency hearing loss) – uncommon with modern preparations, risk increases with cumulative dose and co‑administration of other ototoxins (e.g. gentamicin)  
  - Neutropenia (usually after >2 weeks therapy) – reversible on cessation

### Prognosis
- Vancomycin is effective but **time‑dependent** – maintaining trough >10 mg/L correlates with improved outcomes for MRSA bloodstream infections. Trough >20 mg/L increases AKI risk. Mortality for MRSA bacteraemia ~20–30% with appropriate therapy, higher if source control inadequate.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always use actual body weight for the first dose** – underdosing is a common error in obese patients; after loading, you can adjust based on levels.  
- **Give a loading dose in critically ill patients** – this achieves therapeutic levels faster (by 12–24 hours) and reduces time to clinical response.  
- **Check a trough before the 4th dose** – this is the least disruptive way to get a steady‑state level; if the patient is unstable, check an earlier level (e.g. after 2–3 doses) and [check local policy].

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT skip the loading dose in severe sepsis** – you will have sub‑therapeutic levels for >12 hours.  
- **Do NOT wait for the trough result before giving the second dose** – the first dose is given, and the second dose may be due before the level is back. Give it unless you suspect toxicity (check clinical signs, creatinine).  
- **Do NOT use trough monitoring alone if you switch to continuous infusion** – for continuous infusion the target is a steady‑state level of ~15–25 mg/L, not a pre‑dose trough – consult local pharmacy.  
- **Do NOT co‑administer with other nephrotoxic drugs (gentamicin, furosemide, NSAIDs) without intensified monitoring** – risk of AKI increases.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home on IV Vancomycin)
- **Return if:**  
  - Any new rash, fever, or hypotension during infusion (may be red man syndrome or allergic reaction)  
  - Decreased urine output (oliguria), dark urine, or oedema (may indicate AKI)  
  - Tinnitus, hearing loss, or dizziness  
  - Signs of line sepsis (redness, pain, swelling at IV site)  
- **Follow‑up:**  
  - Weekly U&Es and trough levels (or as per local TDM service)  
  - Weekly FBC if on therapy >7 days  
  - Audiology referral if ototoxic symptoms develop

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior clinician or clinical pharmacist immediately for:**  
  - Trough >20 mg/L (requires dose withholding and review)  
  - Creatinine rise >50% from baseline (or >26 μmol/L in 24 h)  
  - NEWS2 ≥5 (new hypotension, tachypnoea)  
  - Anaphylaxis to vancomycin (use adrenaline 0.5 mg IM)  
  - GCS <15 or new focal neurology (possible meningitis)  
- **Consider ICU referral if:**  
  - Shock requiring vasopressors despite adequate fluid resuscitation  
  - Rapidly progressing AKI (e.g. need for dialysis)  
  - Refractory seizures (consider vancomycin‑induced encephalopathy – rare but dose‑related)
